title: Merger Control ‚Äî Thresholds & Review
content:
Session 7: Merger review [¬ß¬ß5, 6 and 20(4) and 31(1) of the Competition Act  2002; Item # 2, Schedule 1 
of the Competition (Criteria for Exemption of Combination) Rules  2024; cf 2011 Combination 
Regulation 4 and Schedule 1, Item 1] 
 
136 
 
 
 
Facebook / Jio Combination [2020 | CCI] 
Facts: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Notice ‚Üí Jaadhu‚Äôs proposed acquisition of approximately 9.99% of the fully diluted equity share capital of Jio Platforms 
Limited, a subsidiary of Reliance Industries Limited (RIL) ‚Üí not required to be disclosed BUT a voluntary notice was filed 
under Section 5 read with Regulation 2 of the Combination Regulations, 2024 (exemptions). 
‚û¢‚Äã Jaadhu is a US-based subsidiary of Facebook, listed on NASDAQ, and does not independently operate in India.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The Facebook group globally offers products and services in social networking, instant messaging, digital 
advertising, and immersive technology, including Facebook, Instagram, Messenger, WhatsApp, Oculus, 
Workplace and Portal.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Facebook generates virtually all of its revenue from advertising, operating as a multi-sided platform 
monetising user attention and data. 
‚û¢‚Äã Jio Platforms is a technology-focused subsidiary of RIL, holding 100% shares in Reliance Jio Infocomm Ltd. 
(RJIO) and controlling interests in other digital and technology ventures.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
RJIO is a major telecom service provider in India, with a very large subscriber base. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The proposed transaction involved two distinct but interconnected elements: 
‚û¢‚Äã First, under the Investment Agreement and Shareholders‚Äô Agreement,  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Jaadhu would acquire a minority stake of 9.99% in Jio Platforms;  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Right to appoint one director on Jio‚Äôs Board; 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
One board observer, and  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Affirmative voting rights over specific matters such as buybacks, IPOs, amendments to constitutional 
documents, investments beyond a financial threshold, related-party transactions, borrowings beyond a 
limit, and liquidation.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Jaadhu would also receive access to financial and compliance-related information of Jio Platforms. 
‚û¢‚Äã Second, WhatsApp Inc., another Facebook subsidiary, Jio Platforms, and Reliance Retail Ltd. (RRL) proposed to 
enter into a Master Services Agreement (MSA).  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Under the MSA, WhatsApp would develop an electronic chat interface enabling users to interact with 
JioMart, a newly launched e-commerce platform of RRL aimed at digitising kirana stores and small local 
businesses.  
137 
 
‚óè‚Äã
Users would be able to browse products, communicate with sellers, and eventually make 
payments using WhatsApp Pay, subject to regulatory approvals. 
‚óè‚Äã
For commercial reasons, JioMart proposed to use WhatsApp exclusively as its chat interface and 
not engage other third-party messaging platforms. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The value of the transaction was not disclosed in the order (though it was approximately ‚Çπ43,500 crore), which later became 
relevant in the context of the Deal Value Threshold (DVT) introduced in 2023. 
 
Issues: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether Jaadhu‚Äôs acquisition of a minority but influential stake in Jio Platforms, along with governance and information 
rights, could lead to AAEC in markets where the parties‚Äô activities are horizontally overlapping, vertically related, or 
complementary. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the proposed business collaboration between WhatsApp and JioMart could raise competition concerns in the 
e-commerce and UPI-based digital payments markets. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the combination could enable discriminatory conduct, leveraging of market power, or foreclosure of competitors 
through data integration, preferential access, or network effects. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the transaction posed concerns relating to net neutrality and data-backed market power. 
 
Rule:  Sections 5, 6, 20(4) and 31 of the Competition Act, 2002. 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Approach to AAEC Assessment 
‚û¢‚Äã The competition assessment would focus on whether Jaadhu‚Äôs considerable influential interest, including access to 
information and governance rights, could enable the parties to coordinate or foreclose competition in markets 
where their products or services overlap or are vertically related.  
‚û¢‚Äã Adopted a forward-looking, effects-based approach, sensitive to the dynamic and evolving nature of digital 
markets. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Horizontal Overlaps:  
‚û¢‚Äã Consumer Communication Applications 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The first area of potential horizontal overlap was the market for consumer communication applications, 
particularly instant messaging services.  
‚óè‚Äã
WhatsApp and Messenger (Facebook group) and JioChat (Jio Platforms) all offer free messaging 
services with similar functionalities. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Jaadhu argued that defining a narrow relevant market was unnecessary given the minority nature of the 
acquisition, continued independent operation of the firms, and the broader competition for ‚Äúuser 
attention.‚Äù  
‚óè‚Äã
Without prejudice, it was submitted that even in a narrower market for communication 
applications (as considered in Vinod Gupta v. WhatsApp), no competition concerns arose. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The CCI rejected the notion of an all-encompassing ‚Äúuser attention‚Äù market, observing that consumer 
communication applications serve a distinct functional purpose of interpersonal and group interaction.  
‚óè‚Äã
However, the Commission also recognised that further segmentation based on functionalities 
(text, voice, video) was unnecessary due to technological convergence. Modern OTT platforms 
increasingly provide integrated, multi-functional services, making rigid market segmentation 
artificial. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
WhatsApp, Messenger, and JioChat offer similar features and face competition from several global and 
domestic players including Google (Duo, Hangouts), Apple (iMessage, FaceTime), Telegram, Viber, 
WeChat, Snapchat, and emerging apps.  
‚óè‚Äã
While WhatsApp and Messenger together accounted for approximately 45‚Äì50% market share, 
JioChat held only 0‚Äì5%. 
138 
 
‚óÜ‚Äã
BUT ‚Üí market share alone was not determinative in this sector ‚Üí exhibited low entry 
barriers, rapid innovation, and frequent shifts in user preferences, as evidenced by the 
sudden rise of apps like Hike and Houseparty ‚áí parties lacked incentives and ability to 
engage in anti-competitive conduct in the consumer communication applications 
market. 
‚û¢‚Äã Advertisement Services 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The second horizontal overlap concerned advertisement services, where Facebook is a major player but Jio 
Platforms has only insignificant presence. 
‚óè‚Äã
Jaadhu argued that advertising is a broad market, encompassing both online and offline forms, 
which are substitutable from demand and supply perspectives. 
‚óè‚Äã
Without prejudice, Jaadhu also provided data for narrower markets such as online advertising 
and online display advertising. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The CCI undertook a detailed discussion of advertising markets, distinguishing between search and 
non-search advertisements, and recognising the enhanced targeting capabilities of online ads.  
‚óè‚Äã
However, it ultimately left the relevant market definition open, holding that the proposed 
combination would not raise concerns under any plausible market definition. 
‚û¢‚Äã Jio Platforms‚Äô revenue from advertising was extremely low, constituting less than 1% of its total revenue, even in the 
narrowest possible market.  
‚û¢‚Äã By contrast, while Facebook derives substantial revenue from advertising, the presence of Google as a dominant 
competitor significantly constrained Facebook‚Äôs ability to exercise market power.  
‚û¢‚Äã Consequently, the Commission found no incentive or ability for coordination or foreclosure in advertisement 
markets. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Vertical / Complementary Linkages: WhatsApp‚ÄìJioMart Collaboration 
‚û¢‚Äã E-commerce 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
JioMart was characterised as a new entrant, aiming to digitise kirana stores and enable them to compete 
with large e-commerce platforms AND WhatsApp was only one of several channels through which 
consumers could access JioMart, alongside Jio‚Äôs own websites and platforms.  
‚óè‚Äã
Accepted Jaadhu‚Äôs submission that WhatsApp is not an essential input for e-commerce, as 
platforms like Amazon and Flipkart rely primarily on proprietary interfaces. 
‚óè‚Äã
The Commission noted the presence of strong incumbents such as Amazon and 
Walmart-Flipkart, as well as specialised grocery platforms like BigBasket and Grofers.  
‚óÜ‚Äã
In this competitive landscape, the WhatsApp‚ÄìJioMart collaboration was seen as 
pro-competitive, facilitating entry rather than foreclosing competition. 
‚û¢‚Äã UPI-based Digital Payments 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The key combination-specific concern was the linking of JioMart with WhatsApp Pay. 
‚óè‚Äã
However, it emphasised that WhatsApp Pay was itself a new entrant, subject to regulatory 
approvals, and that consumers retained the freedom to use alternative payment methods, 
including other UPI apps and cash. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The UPI market was characterised as dynamic and rapidly evolving, with strong players such as Google 
Pay, PhonePe, and Paytm.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Restricting payments exclusively to WhatsApp Pay would be commercially irrational during JioMart‚Äôs 
formative phase, as it would reduce liquidity and transaction volumes. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Accordingly, the overall volume of UPI transactions routed through WhatsApp Pay for JioMart was 
unlikely to be significant enough to distort competition. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Net Neutrality and Data Integration 
‚û¢‚Äã Given that Facebook applications rely on telecom networks and RJIO is a major TSP, the Commission examined 
net neutrality concerns.  
‚û¢‚Äã Any preferential treatment of Facebook content on RJIO‚Äôs network would violate TRAI‚Äôs Prohibition of 
Discriminatory Tariffs for Data Services Regulations, 2016, and could attract severe regulatory consequences.  
139 
 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Given the partial nature of the acquisition and strong sectoral regulation, the Commission found no need 
for separate scrutiny. 
‚û¢‚Äã On data sharing, the Commission acknowledged that both parties operate in data-intensive, multi-sided markets, 
raising concerns of data-backed market power.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
However, Jaadhu clarified that data sharing was not the purpose of the transaction, and that only limited 
data would be exchanged for operational purposes under the MSA, without transfer of ownership.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The Commission accepted this assurance but expressly reserved the right to examine any future 
anti-competitive data practices under Sections 3 or 4 of the Act. 
 
Conclusion: The CCI concluded that, considering all factors under Section 20(4), the proposed combination was not likely to cause 
an appreciable adverse effect on competition in India. Accordingly, the transaction was approved under Section 31(1). The 
Commission also noted the absence of any non-compete obligations. 
 
Google‚ÄìJio Combination Order [2020 | CCI] 
Facts: 
‚ùñ‚Äã The notice was filed by Google LLC in relation to its proposed acquisition of approximately 7.73% of the equity share 
capital of Jio Platforms Limited, a subsidiary of Reliance Industries Limited (RIL). 
‚û¢‚Äã Google invested in Jio Platforms under an Investment Agreement dated 14 July 2020.  
‚û¢‚Äã Along with acquiring a minority stake, Google received limited governance and information rights, including 
board representation and access to specified financial and compliance information.  
‚û¢‚Äã Separately, Google group entities entered into a Foundational Commercial Agreement with Jio Platforms to 
collaborate on developing low-cost smartphones and a customized operating system for the Indian market. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The proposed transaction thus had a dual character: first, a minority but influential equity investment by Google in Jio 
Platforms; and second, a strategic vertical and complementary collaboration between the two groups in areas such as 
smartphones, operating systems, applications, data, and digital services. Importantly, the parties maintained that they would 
continue to operate independently post-combination and that no full integration of businesses was contemplated. 
 
Issues: Whether Google‚Äôs acquisition of a considerable influential interest in Jio Platforms, coupled with strategic commercial 
collaboration, was likely to result in an AAEC in India, particularly in markets where the parties‚Äô products and services were 
horizontal overlaps, vertically related, or complementary, including: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Online advertising services 
‚ùñ‚Äã Supply of apps and digital services 
‚ùñ‚Äã Mobile operating systems 
‚ùñ‚Äã Smartphone markets 
‚ùñ‚Äã Net neutrality in telecom services 
‚ùñ‚Äã Potential anticompetitive data sharing and leveraging 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Online Advertising Services 
‚û¢‚Äã The Commission first examined the online advertising market, where Google enjoys a dominant position, while Jio 
Platforms has a relatively insignificant presence.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Given this asymmetry, the proposed combination did not create incentives for horizontal coordination or 
collusion in any plausible online advertising market. 
‚û¢‚Äã Importantly, the Commission adopted a structural incentive-based analysis, observing that Google already 
operates independently at scale in online advertising and does not meaningfully benefit from coordinating with a 
relatively minor advertising player like Jio Platforms.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
As such, the transaction did not materially alter market structure or competitive dynamics in online 
advertising. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Supply of Apps and Mobile/Web-Based Digital Services 
140 
 
‚û¢‚Äã The Commission next examined overlaps in the supply of applications and digital services, noting that the parties 
offered similar services in domains such as mobile browsers, payment services, video streaming, music and radio 
streaming, communication services, cloud storage, news, education, and gaming. 
‚û¢‚Äã However, the combined market share of the parties in each of these segments was below 30%, a threshold that 
typically does not raise prima facie concerns.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
While acknowledging that market share is not determinative in digital markets, the Commission treated it 
as a starting point for inquiry. 
‚û¢‚Äã Crucially, the CCI emphasised that these were ‚Äúnew age, dynamic markets‚Äù, characterised by multi-homing, low 
switching costs, rapid innovation, and contestability.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
In such markets, users can easily switch between competing applications, and firms face constant 
competitive pressure.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Consequently, the Commission found no realistic likelihood of market power consolidation or 
exclusionary conduct arising merely from the proposed collaboration. 
‚óè‚Äã
This reflects a non-interventionist, Chicago-style approach, where the Commission refrained 
from speculative theories of harm absent clear foreclosure incentives or effects. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Mobile Operating Systems (Android and KaiOS) 
‚û¢‚Äã A significant concern examined by the Commission was whether the collaboration on new smartphones and 
operating systems would reduce incentives for Reliance Retail Limited (RRL) to continue supporting KaiOS-based 
feature phones, thereby favouring Android-based smartphones developed under the collaboration. 
‚û¢‚Äã The parties submitted categorical assurances that RRL would continue to sell KaiOS devices and would not 
degrade their quality or availability to favour the new smartphones ‚Üí accepted these assurances and found no 
evidence suggesting that the collaboration would distort competition in the mobile OS market. 
‚û¢‚Äã Further, the CCI rejected the argument that Google would have incentives to restrict Android access or degrade 
Android quality for rival OEMs ‚Üí Google‚Äôs primary revenue stream is advertising, which depends on widespread 
Android adoption across devices and manufacturers. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Any foreclosure strategy would thus be economically irrational. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Market for New Smartphones 
‚û¢‚Äã The Commission observed that the smartphones to be developed under the collaboration were yet to enter the 
market, and that the Indian smartphone market is highly competitive, with established players such as Samsung, 
Vivo, Xiaomi, Oppo, Lava, and others. 
‚û¢‚Äã Outside the collaboration, neither Google nor Jio Platforms had a significant presence in smartphone 
manufacturing or supply.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Given the absence of market power, high levels of competition, and uncertainty surrounding market 
entry, the Commission found no likelihood of AAEC in this market. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Net Neutrality Concerns 
‚û¢‚Äã A central vertical concern raised was whether the proposed combination could lead to preferential treatment of 
Google‚Äôs applications or content on RJIO‚Äôs telecom network, thereby violating net neutrality principles. 
‚û¢‚Äã The Commission undertook a contextual analysis of India‚Äôs telecom regulatory framework and noted that any 
preferential treatment of OTT services [such as faster access, lower data costs, or prioritised traffic] would be 
expressly prohibited under existing license conditions and TRAI regulations. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Accepted Google‚Äôs submission that violations of net neutrality obligations could result in severe penalties, 
including revocation of RJIO‚Äôs telecom license, thereby significantly deterring any discriminatory 
conduct.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Moreover, the transaction documents contained no clauses mandating or enabling preferential treatment. 
‚û¢‚Äã Accordingly, the CCI concluded that concerns relating to net neutrality were already adequately addressed by 
sectoral regulation, and did not warrant ex ante competition law intervention. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Potential Data Sharing and Data Concentration 
‚û¢‚Äã The transaction involved only a 7.73% minority stake, and did not result in unrestricted access to each other‚Äôs data 
resources ‚Üí rejected any assumption of full data integration or merger of data banks. 
141 
 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Google further submitted that the collaboration did not contemplate sharing pre-existing proprietary 
datasets, and that any data accessed would be limited to what is necessary for legitimate collaboration 
purposes, subject to applicable data protection laws, privacy policies, and user consent. 
‚û¢‚Äã Nevertheless, in a move consistent with its approach in the Facebook‚ÄìJio order, the Commission issued a clear 
warning that any future anticompetitive conduct arising from data sharing or leveraging could be examined 
independently under Sections 3 and 4 of the Act, irrespective of the present approval. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
This reflects a post-approval enforcement posture, preserving regulatory oversight without blocking the 
transaction. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ùñ‚Äã After considering the material on record and applying the factors under Section 20(4), the CCI concluded that the proposed 
combination was not likely to cause any AAEC. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Accordingly, the Commission approved the transaction under Section 31(1) of the Act, while expressly reserving its powers 
to scrutinise any future anticompetitive conduct arising from the collaboration. 
 
Reliance ‚Äì Disney Merger [2024 | CCI] 
Facts: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Viacom18 Media Private Limited and The Walt Disney Company (TWDC).  
‚û¢‚Äã Viacom18 is jointly held by Reliance Industries Limited (RIL), TV18 Broadcast Limited, and Bodhi Tree Systems 
(BTS).  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Viacom18 also has a wholly owned subsidiary, Digital18, and operates a large portfolio of approximately 
20 international linear TV channel feeds, including Colors, MTV India International, Colors Rishtey, 
Colors Cineplex, and multiple regional channels, which are distributed across several countries. 
‚û¢‚Äã TWDC is a globally integrated media and entertainment enterprise headquartered in the United States, with 
business segments spanning entertainment, sports, and experiences.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
In India, TWDC operates primarily through Star India Private Limited (SIPL), which holds a substantial 
presence in linear television, sports broadcasting, and OTT streaming through Disney+ Hotstar. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The proposed combination envisaged the merger of Viacom18‚Äôs and Disney‚Äôs entertainment businesses in India, subject to 
certain inclusions and exclusions, through a series of interconnected transactions. 
‚û¢‚Äã Pursuant to the transaction, Star India (SIPL) would become a joint venture (JV) / resultant entity, housing the 
digital and linear television entertainment businesses of both Viacom18 and TWDC in India. 
‚ùñ‚Äã A key commercial motivation underlying the transaction was Reliance‚Äôs strategic interest in sports broadcasting rights 
[specially IPL] which attract massive viewership and enable high-value advertising monetisation.  
‚û¢‚Äã Given the growing centrality of OTT platforms and sports content, the merger raised significant competition 
concerns across broadcasting, OTT streaming, and advertising markets. 
 
Issues: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the proposed combination would result in an Appreciable Adverse Effect on Competition (AAEC) in India, 
considering: 
‚û¢‚Äã Horizontal overlaps in: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Operation and wholesale supply of TV channels 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Retail supply of AV content through OTT platforms 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Supply of advertising airtime 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Licensing of audio-visual (AV) content 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Distribution of broadcast TV channels 
‚û¢‚Äã Vertical overlaps and complementary linkages, including: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Licensing of AV content (upstream) and its downstream distribution via TV and OTT 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Supply of advertisement space and downstream advertising services 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
TV channel operation and downstream distribution 
142 
 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Internet access services (fixed and wireless) complementing OTT platforms 
‚ùñ‚Äã Specific concerns in sports broadcasting, particularly cricket (IPL), where exclusivity, high concentration, and bidding 
dynamics could enhance market power. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the voluntary modifications (VPM I and VPM II) offered by the Parties sufficiently mitigated these concerns. 
 
Rules: S.5 ‚Äì clause (f) of explanation [acquisition of division of a company], 6, 20(4) + Regulation 19(2) and Regulation 25(1A), 26 
of the Combination Regulations 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Horizontal Overlaps 
‚û¢‚Äã Operation and Wholesale Supply of TV Channels 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Viacom18 and Star India are both active in the operation and wholesale supply of TV channels in India, 
while TWDC itself has no independent broadcasting activity in India outside Star India.  
‚óè‚Äã
The Indian television market is structurally broad, with approximately 300 broadcasters offering 
over 900 TV channels across genres and languages. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Although the Parties initially submitted that further segmentation was unnecessary, the Commission 
adopted a narrower analytical approach, examining TV channels by genre (GEC, films, sports, kids, 
infotainment) and, where relevant, by language.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Third parties raised concerns that the merged entity would gain the ability and incentive to raise prices for 
advertisers and consumers. 
‚óè‚Äã
These concerns were substantially addressed through Voluntary Proposal for Modifications I 
(VPM I), which involved: 
‚óÜ‚Äã
Divestment of certain TV channels, and 
‚óÜ‚Äã
Assignment of rights in ETPL, ensuring continued competition. 
‚óè‚Äã
As a result, overlaps in sensitive segments such as Bengali films and Marathi GEC ceased entirely, 
while in other segments, market shares were reduced and competitive constraints continued to 
exist.  
‚óÜ‚Äã
Importantly, the Commission noted that ETPL would remain competitively 
independent, with no direct or indirect influence from the RIL group. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
In segments such as Hindi GEC and Hindi films, either the incremental market share was insignificant or 
sufficient competitive alternatives remained ‚Üí no AAEC arising from horizontal overlaps in TV 
broadcasting. 
‚û¢‚Äã Retail Supply of AV Content through OTT Streaming Platforms 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The Commission examined the OTT streaming market, recognising it as a distinct and rapidly evolving 
mode of AV content consumption, characterised by flexibility, on-demand viewing, and access across 
devices. OTT platforms employ diverse monetisation models‚Äîadvertisement-based, subscription-based, 
transactional, and hybrid. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The Parties submitted, and the Commission accepted, that segmentation of OTT markets by genre or 
language was inappropriate, since platforms offer bundled content libraries and do not price subscriptions 
on a per-genre or per-language basis.  
‚óè‚Äã
Accordingly, the analysis was conducted at the overall OTT streaming market level. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The absence of a single universally accepted metric for OTT market power, and therefore considered 
multiple indicators, including monthly active users (MAU), average weekly watch time (AWWT), and 
advertising/subscription revenues. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
While concerns were raised regarding sports content, particularly cricket, the Commission noted that: 
‚óè‚Äã
Much of the IPL content was streamed free of charge on JioCinema, 
‚óè‚Äã
Advertising revenues did not fully offset the high costs of acquiring sports rights, 
‚óè‚Äã
Excessive increases in advertisement rates would likely divert viewers and advertisers to rival 
platforms. 
143 
 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Additionally, VPM II imposed commitments restricting unreasonable increases in advertising and 
subscription pricing, further alleviating concerns ‚Üí no AAEC arose in the OTT streaming market. 
‚û¢‚Äã Supply of Advertising Airtime in India 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The advertising market involves suppliers of advertising space (TV channels, OTT platforms), advertising 
agencies, and advertisers.  
‚óè‚Äã
While the Parties argued that advertising across different media forms a part of a broader ‚Äúmedia 
mix,‚Äù pricing data and third-party submissions indicated that TV and digital advertising cannot 
be treated as perfectly interchangeable. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Third parties expressed concerns that the merged entity could increase advertising rates across TV and 
OTT platforms.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
However, the Commission accepted the Parties‚Äô submissions that: 
‚óè‚Äã
Large advertisers and advertising agencies possess significant countervailing bargaining power, 
and 
‚óè‚Äã
A substantial proportion of advertising airtime on both TV and OTT platforms remains 
unutilised, indicating supply-side constraints on price increases. 
‚óè‚Äã
Further, the divestments under VPM I reduced combined market shares in TV advertising, 
while VPM II specifically addressed advertising concerns in sports broadcasting. The 
Commission held that these measures sufficiently mitigated any risk of AAEC. 
‚û¢‚Äã Licensing of AV Content 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The incremental market share of the Parties in the licensing of AV content [both film and non-film] was 
not significant enough to raise competition concerns.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Adequate alternative licensors and buyers continued to exist, preventing foreclosure. 
‚û¢‚Äã Distribution of Broadcast TV Channels 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
In the downstream market for distribution of TV channels, the Commission observed that the combined 
market share of the Parties was approximately 20‚Äì25%, with an incremental increase of around 10%.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The market remained highly fragmented, with competitors such as DD Free Dish, Bharti Telemedia, Sun 
Direct, Dish TV, numerous MSOs and LCOs, IPTV operators, and an MTS operator. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Regulatory safeguards such as must-carry obligations and quality-of-service regulations ensured 
non-discriminatory access and prevented unilateral degradation of service quality ‚Üí no AAEC was found 
in this segment. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Vertical Overlaps and Complementary Linkages 
‚û¢‚Äã The Commission examined vertical linkages across AV content licensing, advertising supply, TV channel 
operation, and distribution, as well as complementary linkages between RIL‚Äôs broadband/internet services and 
OTT platforms. 
‚û¢‚Äã Based on market shares, structural conditions, and the voluntary commitments, the Commission concluded that 
these vertical relationships did not give rise to input foreclosure or customer foreclosure concerns. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Sports Broadcasting ‚Äì Prima Facie Concerns and Resolution 
‚û¢‚Äã The most serious concerns arose in sports broadcasting, particularly cricket.  
‚û¢‚Äã The Commission noted: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
High concentration in sports TV channels (delta HHI > 200), 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Exclusive ownership of key cricket rights by the Parties, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Limited substitutability between cricket and other sports, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Unique market structure created by the splitting of broadcasting and streaming rights. 
‚û¢‚Äã Given the potential for increased negotiating power and monetisation via subscriptions and advertising, the 
Commission issued a show cause notice, prima facie finding a likelihood of AAEC. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
However, the Parties‚Äô commitments under VPM II addressed these concerns by: 
‚óè‚Äã
Restricting unreasonable increases in advertising rates, 
‚óè‚Äã
Ensuring subscription pricing aligned with industry standards, 
‚óè‚Äã
Limiting bundling and self-preferencing practices, 
144 
 
‚óè‚Äã
Ensuring fair and reasonable conduct for the duration of existing sports rights cycles. 
‚û¢‚Äã The Commission also emphasised the dynamic, bidding-based nature of sports broadcasting markets, the role of 
TRAI in regulating subscription prices, and the fact that OTT growth was driven largely by free IPL streaming. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ùñ‚Äã After considering the material on record, the factors under Section 20(4), and the voluntary commitments offered under 
Regulations 19(2) and 25(1A), the Commission concluded that the proposed combination was not likely to result in any 
AAEC in India. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Accordingly, the CCI approved the Reliance‚ÄìDisney (Viacom18‚ÄìStar India) merger under Section 31(1) of the 
Competition Act, subject to compliance with the commitments. 
 
CCI v. Thomas Cook (India) Ltd. [2018 | SC | 2J] 
Facts: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Appeal arose from an order of the NCLAT setting aside the penalty imposed by the CCI on Thomas Cook (India) Limited 
(TCIL) and its group entity Thomas Cook Insurance Services India Limited (TCISIL).  
‚û¢‚Äã The CCI challenged this decision, contending that the respondents had violated the Combination Regulations by 
failing to notify a composite combination in its entirety and by consummating parts of the transaction prior to 
CCI approval ‚Üí amounting to gun-jumping. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The target enterprise was Sterling Hotels and Resorts India Limited (SHRIL), while Thomas Cook entities were the 
acquirers.  
‚û¢‚Äã The transaction structure envisaged a composite combination, formally notified to the CCI, consisting of two 
principal notified transactions: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Demerger: The resorts and timeshare business of SHRIL was to be demerged and transferred to TCISIL. 
In return, equity shares of TCIL were to be issued to the shareholders of SHRIL. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Amalgamation: SHRIL‚Äôs residual business was to be amalgamated into TCIL, again in exchange for 
equity shares issued to SHRIL shareholders. 
‚û¢‚Äã These transactions were implemented pursuant to a Merger Cooperation Agreement (MCA) executed in 2014. 
‚û¢‚Äã In addition to these notified steps, the Thomas Cook entities undertook multiple other transactions ‚Äì including a 
Share Subscription Agreement (SSA), Share Purchase Agreements (SPA), an open offer, and market purchases of 
SHRIL shares ‚Äì which were disclosed to the CCI but not notified as part of the combination.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Crucially, these market purchases were executed between 10‚Äì12 February 2014, before notice under 
Section 6(2) was filed on 14 February 2014. 
‚ùñ‚Äã While the CCI approved the notified combination under Section 31(1), it initiated penalty proceedings under Section 43A, 
holding that the respondents had consummated a part of the composite combination prior to notification and approval. 
 
Issues: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the failure to notify the market purchases and other ancillary transactions as part of the same combination violated 
the Combination Regulations, particularly Regulations 9(4) and 9(5). 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the non-notified transactions were independent transactions or inter-connected and inter-dependent steps of a 
single composite combination. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether consummation of such transactions prior to CCI approval constituted gun-jumping under the Competition Act. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether mens rea or mala fide intent is a necessary requirement for imposing penalty under Section 43A. 
 
Rules: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Sections 5 and 6, Competition Act, 2002 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 5 defines a combination to include: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Section 5(a): Acquisition of shares, voting rights, assets, or control, subject to thresholds. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Section 5(b): Acquisition of control where the acquirer already controls another enterprise in a similar or 
substitutable market. 
145 
 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Section 5(c): Mergers and amalgamations crossing asset/turnover thresholds. 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 6(2) mandates prior notification of combinations and imposes a standstill obligation until approval or lapse 
of 210 days. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Combination Regulations 
‚û¢‚Äã Regulation 9(4) (then applicable): Requires a single notice where the ultimate intended effect of a transaction is 
achieved through inter-connected or inter-dependent steps. 
‚û¢‚Äã Regulation 9(5): Prevents parties from fragmenting a combination to avoid notification. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 43A ‚Äì empowers the CCI to impose a penalty (up to 1% of total turnover/assets) for failure to notify a combination 
or for gun-jumping. 
‚û¢‚Äã Does not require proof of mens rea. 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Nature of the Transactions: Composite and Inter-Connected 
‚û¢‚Äã SC undertook a substance-over-form analysis  
‚û¢‚Äã Agreed with the CCI that the demerger, amalgamation, SSA, SPA, open offer, and market purchases were not 
independent transactions, but rather inter-connected and inter-dependent steps forming a single composite 
combination. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
First, all transactions were contemporaneous, having been conceptualised, approved, and executed within 
a narrow time window.  
‚óè‚Äã
The scheme, board resolutions, and authorisations for market purchases were all undertaken on 
the same day, strongly indicating a unified business strategy. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Secondly, the joint press release dated 7 February 2014 explicitly referred to the SPA and open offer as part 
of a single transaction framework.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Thirdly, the fact that market purchases were expressly referred to in the Section 6(2) notice itself 
undermined the claim that they were independent. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The Court rejected the argument that the absence of express references to market purchases in the MCA or scheme 
insulated them from being part of the combination ‚Üí formal documentation cannot override factual and economic reality.  
‚û¢‚Äã The decisive question was whether the market purchases would have occurred in the absence of the scheme ‚Üí and 
the Court answered this in the negative. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Regulation 9(4): The Ultimate Intended Effect Test 
‚û¢‚Äã Applying Regulation 9(4), the Court endorsed the ‚Äúultimate intended effect‚Äù test, holding that where a business 
objective is achieved through multiple inter-related steps, the law requires them to be notified together. 
‚û¢‚Äã Technical compartmentalisation of transactions would defeat the object of merger control. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Allowing parties to notify only threshold-crossing transactions while consummating others in advance 
would enable strategic gun-jumping, rendering Section 6 nugatory. 
‚û¢‚Äã The market purchases were consummated after finalisation of the scheme but before notification, clearly indicating 
that they were steps towards implementation of the combination, not standalone investments. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Rejection of the Target Exemption Argument 
‚û¢‚Äã Thomas Cook contended that the market purchases were exempt under the target exemption notification, as 
SHRIL individually fell below asset/turnover thresholds ‚Äì REJECTED ‚Üí threshold analysis applies to the 
transaction as a whole, not to artificially disaggregated steps. 
‚û¢‚Äã When a series of transactions is envisaged to achieve a combination, the CCI must consider all steps cumulatively, 
and not in isolation.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Exemptions cannot be invoked selectively for individual steps of a composite combination.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Structuring transactions to fall below thresholds, when viewed separately, was held to be impermissible 
circumvention of the Act. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Gun-Jumping and Violation of Section 6(2) 
‚û¢‚Äã Consummation of market purchases before notification and approval constituted a clear breach of the standstill 
obligation under Section 6(2).  
146 
 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The prohibition on giving effect to a combination prior to approval applies not merely to the final step 
but to any step that advances implementation of the combination. 
‚û¢‚Äã By acquiring shares in the market between 10‚Äì12 February 2014 [before filing notice on 14 February 2014] they 
had jumped the gun, even though formal control had not yet passed. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Mens Rea Not Required under Section 43A 
‚û¢‚Äã On the question of penalty, mens rea or mala fide intent is not a prerequisite for imposing penalty under Section 
43A ‚Üí imposes liability for breach of a civil obligation, not for criminal or quasi-criminal wrongdoing. 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 43A does not employ language requiring willfulness or intention.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Once a statutory violation is established, penalty follows simpliciter, with discretion only as to quantum, 
not liability. 
‚û¢‚Äã Accordingly, the respondents‚Äô plea of bona fide belief or absence of malice was rejected. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Quantum of Penalty 
‚û¢‚Äã The CCI had imposed a nominal penalty of ‚Çπ1 crore, despite being statutorily empowered to levy up to 1% of total 
turnover ‚Üí upheld this penalty, observing that the Commission had exercised restraint and proportionality, taking 
into account the nature of the breach. 
 
Conclusion: SC allowed the appeal of the CCI, set aside the NCLAT order, and restored the penalty imposed on Thomas Cook. It 
held that: 
‚ùñ‚Äã The transactions formed a single composite combination, 
‚ùñ‚Äã Non-notification and pre-approval consummation amounted to gun-jumping, 
‚ùñ‚Äã Regulation 9(4) mandates notification of all inter-connected steps, 
‚ùñ‚Äã Mens rea is irrelevant for penalty under Section 43A.
